The document is ordered concept-first, with scannable schemas and valid/invalid examples.

The specification is complete enough for parser, linter, author, evaluator, and report-renderer implementation. Scalar bounds remain intentionally broad, so the result satisfies the requirement without reaching outstanding.

findingSummaries.summary: The document structure, frontmatter schema, body semantics, evaluation semantics, report semantics, and extensions are all defined in one specification.
Model, Rating Scale, Target, Factor, and Requirement shapes define requiredness, cardinality, inheritance/default source behavior, and malformed-value handling.
Scalar placeholders such as <string> and <level-name> intentionally do not define detailed character-set or length bounds, leaving edge cases to conforming tools.
BCP 14 keywords are scoped to all-capital uses, reducing ambiguity around obligation strength.
Examples and notes are explicitly non-normative and do not add conformance requirements.
The not-assessed boundary still depends on evaluator judgment about evidence sufficiency, but the report distinction is explicit.
The specification states that all content is normative except passages explicitly marked otherwise.
Appendix A, Appendix B, and Appendix C are each explicitly non-normative before their examples.
Quality Model, Entity, Model, Target, Source, Factor, Requirement, Assessment, Finding, Rating Scale, Rating Level, Rating Result, and Evaluation Report are defined before schema rules.
Later schema and evaluation sections reuse those defined terms consistently.
Rating Level rules require level, title, and criterion; the suggested scale and minimal example now include those fields.
Appendix B now includes title fields for the target and unacceptable rating levels and for the reliability factor.
Frontmatter validity, required properties, list cardinality, factor references, and assessment scalar shape are all observable from the document.
Roll-up intentionally has no numeric aggregation formula, so exact rating inference remains evaluator judgment constrained by required distinctions.
Appendix A and Appendix B provide copyable valid examples for the rating scale and a minimal file.
Appendix C provides worked invalid counter-examples for a missing rating-level title, direct target requirement without factors, and list-valued assessment.
The root model requires ratingScale plus at least one of factors, requirements, or targets.
Extensions cannot change conforming-property meaning, and tools should preserve unrecognized extension fields and body content.
The specification identifies its version and points to the project versioning policy for forward-versioning behavior.
Terminology precedes document structure, schema, and evaluation semantics.
Annotated YAML blocks make required fields and nesting shapes quick to scan.
Valid and invalid appendices give copy-and-adapt examples after the normative sections.
The first paragraph says QUALITY.md is an agent-friendly file format with companion skill and CLI for coding-agent and AI assistant projects/harnesses.
The Why section frames the problem as technical, cognitive, and intent debt becoming explicit and checkable.
A realistic Support Inbox QUALITY.md excerpt shows ratingScale, target source, factors, requirements, and body context.
The README shows the evaluation report build output and a report-summary excerpt, making the CLI payoff visible rather than only described.
Install remains a two-step skill and CLI install sequence.
The Usage section gives `qualitymd init` then `qualitymd lint`, followed by expected output showing `Created QUALITY.md` and `QUALITY.md is valid.`
The CLI section lists current commands: init, lint, spec, status, version, upgrade, and the evaluation run-record surface.
The typical local loop uses existing commands: spec, init, lint, and status --json.
The current command tree exposes the commands named in README.md.
evaluation status now emits a repository-relative `path`, repository-relative next action, and `missing-root-analysis` for an empty run.
lint '-' now maps to the usage category; go run reports `exit status 2`, matching the CLI exit-code contract.
Focused regression tests pass for CLI behavior, evaluation record/report behavior, and status snapshots.
The no-argument root command renders concise examples-first welcome output rather than full help.
The evaluation surface follows a consistent noun/verb tree: create, list, status, assessment-result add/list, analysis set/list, recommendation add/list, report build/gate.
Human styling is conditional on terminal output and NO_COLOR, keeping redirected output plain.
